title: When Fixing One Qubit Breaks Two

color_semantics.crimson: error propagation / two data-qubit errors / code failure

metadata.note: Exclusions: no stabilizer group formalism, no surface-code lattice, no threshold theorem formula, no magic-state distillation

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A three-qubit bit-flip code can fix any single qubit error. You flip one qubit by accident, the parity check catches it, you fix it. The logical qubit survives.

props.headline: A three-qubit bit-flip code can fix any single qubit error. You…

card.copy:
3-qubit code:
fix any single qubit error.

beats.narration_text: To catch the error, you measure parity. An ancilla qubit applies CNOT to qubit one, then CNOT to qubit two. It reads out whether the pair has flipped. One ancilla, two data qubits, two gates.
beats.new_visual_element: [CIRCUIT] ancilla with CNOT to Q1 then CNOT to Q2

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CIRCUIT] ancilla with CNOT to Q1 then CNOT to Q2

beats.narration_text: Now the ancilla itself has an error at gate one. A single gate fails. The code was designed to handle single errors. This should be fine. But it is not.
beats.new_visual_element: [CIRCUIT] same circuit with X error on ancilla at gate 1

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CIRCUIT] same circuit with X error on ancilla at gate 1

beats.narration_text: A single ancilla gate fails. The 3-qubit code is designed to correct any single-qubit error. Why does one gate failure break a code that should handle one error?

props.headline: A single ancilla gate fails. The 3-qubit code is designed to…

card.copy:
Why does one gate failure break
a code designed for one error?

beats.new_visual_element: [QUESTION] why one gate failure breaks the code

beats.narration_text: Because the ancilla is not a bystander.

props.headline: Because the ancilla is not a bystander.

beats.narration_text: After the first CNOT, the ancilla is in an error state. But it has not finished its job. Gate two — CNOT from corrupted ancilla to qubit two — now copies the ancilla's error onto qubit two. One ancilla error becomes two data-qubit errors.
beats.new_visual_element: [CIRCUIT] error on ancilla propagating through gate 2 to Q2

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CIRCUIT] error on ancilla propagating through gate 2 to Q2

beats.narration_text: The three-qubit code corrects errors on one qubit at a time. When it sees two data qubits flipped, it applies the wrong correction. The logical qubit is not saved — it fails. One physical gate error causes a logical qubit failure.
beats.new_visual_element: [TABLE] 2-qubit error vs. code correction table: wrong outcome

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TABLE] 2-qubit error vs. code correction table: wrong outcome

beats.narration_text: The distinction: error correction versus fault tolerance.

props.headline: The distinction: error correction versus fault tolerance.

beats.narration_text: Error correction handles errors on data qubits. Fault tolerance is a separate requirement: a single error anywhere in the syndrome circuit — on any gate, any ancilla — must propagate to at most one data-qubit error. Fault tolerance requires the syndrome circuit to be redesigned.
beats.new_visual_element: [COMPARE] error correction vs fault tolerance side-by-side

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [COMPARE] error correction vs fault tolerance side-by-side

beats.narration_text: Use one ancilla per stabilizer instead of one ancilla for both. Each ancilla touches only one data qubit. Now a single ancilla error can corrupt only one data qubit. The code corrects one error — and now it always has at most one to correct.
beats.new_visual_element: [CIRCUIT] fault-tolerant design: two ancillas, each touches one data qubit

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CIRCUIT] fault-tolerant design: two ancillas, each touches one data qubit

beats.narration_text: A team deploys a three-qubit code and sees logical error rates three times higher than expected. Investigation: their ancilla measures both parity stabilizers sequentially. One ancilla error spreads to two data qubits. They add a second ancilla — one per stabilizer. Logical error rates fall to predicted levels. The fix costs one extra qubit.
beats.new_visual_element: [DIAGRAM] before/after: one ancilla vs two ancillas, error rates

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [DIAGRAM] before/after: one ancilla vs two ancillas, error rates

beats.narration_text: A code that corrects one error will fail if its syndrome circuit lets one gate error become two data errors. Fault tolerance means: one circuit-level error, at most one data-qubit consequence.

props.headline: A code that corrects one error will fail if its syndrome…

card.copy:
Error correction: fix data qubit errors.
Fault tolerance: no single gate error
spreads to two data qubits.
